rad_findings_en:
Lungs and pleura: Pulmonary nodules in series 5 are as follows:

Right middle lobe:
- Centrilobular micronodules measuring up to 3 mm on images 102-118.

In the left upper lobe, there is a subpleural area of consolidation, series 5, images 110-139. In the left lower lobe, there is a subpleural area of consolidation, series 5, images 88-119. In the right middle lobe, bronchiectasis is present in the setting of the above-described areas of consolidation. In both lungs, there are solid micronodules measuring up to 3 mm. In both lungs, there is mild paraseptal emphysema. In both lungs, there are discoid atelectases. In the right lower lobe, there is a calcified granuloma measuring up to 3 mm. In the left pleural space, there is a small amount of effusion with a layer thickness up to 1.5 cm, volume about 116 mL. Bronchial walls are unevenly thickened, with bronchial contents in the lumens of some bronchi (predominantly at the level of the right middle and lower lobes).

Mediastinum and lung hila: No mass lesion or lymphadenopathy is identified. At the level of the mid third of the esophagus along the right semicircumference, there is a wall diverticulum measuring up to 2.4 × 1.6 cm.

Heart: No cardiomegaly is identified. No clinically significant pericardial effusion. Coronary calcification, qualitatively assessed as severe. Coronary artery bypass grafting.

Great vessels: The thoracic aorta is not dilated, with moderate wall calcification. The main pulmonary artery is not dilated.

Skeletal structures and chest wall: Moderate degenerative changes of the thoracic spine. Diffuse uneven decreased density of vertebral bodies (maximally up to 72 HU). Sternotomy.

Upper abdomen: In the liver, there is a calcification measuring up to 6 mm. In the gallbladder lumen, there is wall-adherent dense contents.
rad_impression_en:
1.

Micronodules in both lungs. Per 2017 Fleischner Society recommendations, in high-risk patients follow-up chest CT in 12 months is recommended; in low-risk patients, no follow-up is required. Centrilobular micronodules in the right middle lobe may correspond to bronchiolitis. Areas of consolidation in the left lung require differentiation between sequelae of prior inflammatory changes versus the course of left-sided polysegmental pneumonia. Bronchial secretions and bronchial wall thickening in both lungs, which may correspond to bronchitis. Mild paraseptal emphysema in both lungs. Bronchiectasis and discoid atelectasis in both lungs. Calcified granuloma in the right lower lobe, consistent with prior infection.

2.

Small-volume left pleural effusion.

3.

Esophageal wall diverticulum.

4.

Moderate atherosclerosis of the thoracic aorta.

5.

Coronary calcification, qualitatively assessed as severe. Coronary artery bypass grafting.

6.

Findings consistent with osteoporosis. Sternotomy.

7.

Hepatic calcification; no further workup is required.

8.

Dense contents in the gallbladder lumen.

DISCLAIMER: AI lung nodule detection was utilized on this study. This system is designed for high sensitivity, potentially identifying benign findings. Please refer to the radiologist's interpretation to assess the clinical significance of any detected findings.

Lungs and pleura: Pulmonary nodules in series 5 are as follows:

Right middle lobe:
- Centrilobular micronodules measuring up to 3 mm on images 102-118.

In the left upper lobe, there is a subpleural area of consolidation, series 5, images 110-139. In the left lower lobe, there is a subpleural area of consolidation, series 5, images 88-119. In the right middle lobe, bronchiectasis is present in the setting of the above-described areas of consolidation. In both lungs, there are solid micronodules measuring up to 3 mm. In both lungs, there is mild paraseptal emphysema. In both lungs, there are discoid atelectases. In the right lower lobe, there is a calcified granuloma measuring up to 3 mm. In the left pleural space, there is a small amount of effusion with a layer thickness up to 1.5 cm, volume about 116 mL. Bronchial walls are unevenly thickened, with bronchial contents in the lumens of some bronchi (predominantly at the level of the right middle and lower lobes).

Mediastinum and lung hila: No mass lesion or lymphadenopathy is identified. At the level of the mid third of the esophagus along the right semicircumference, there is a wall diverticulum measuring up to 2.4 × 1.6 cm.

Heart: No cardiomegaly is identified, enlargement of the left heart.No clinically significant pericardial effusion. Coronary calcification, qualitatively assessed as severe. Aortic and mitral valves calcifications.

Great vessels: The thoracic aorta is not dilated, with moderate wall calcification. The main pulmonary artery is not dilated.

Skeletal structures and chest wall: Moderate degenerative changes of the thoracic spine. Diffuse decreased density of vertebral bodies (maximally up to 72 HU). Sternotomy.

Upper abdomen: In the liver, there is a calcification measuring up to 6 mm, no further workup is required. Gallbladder sludge.

1.

Areas of consolidation in the left lung require differentiation between sequelae of prior inflammatory changes versus the course of left-sided polysegmental pneumonia. rad_findings_en:
Lungs and pleura: Clustered solid nodules persist in the left lower lobe, forming a “tree-in-bud” pattern, compared with the prior study with decreased size:

- The largest nodule previously up to 1.3 × 0.8 cm (series 5, image 112) has decreased to 6 mm on the current study, is less rounded, and has more linear margins.

- The largest nodule on the current study is up to 8 mm (series 5, image 104), previously up to 9 mm.

The remaining previously noted solid and calcified nodules (single) show no interval change, shown on series 5:

In the right lung:

- In the upper lobe, a subpleural nodule up to 3 mm, partially calcified (image 68).

- In the middle lobe, a subpleural nodule up to 3 mm (image 103).

Single calcifications in the left upper lobe up to 5 mm (no interval change).

No pleural effusion.

Mediastinum and lung hila: A calcified lymph node of station 5 persists (enlarged up to 1.5 cm in short axis, no interval change), with single calcifications in nonenlarged lymph nodes of station 10L (no interval change).

Heart: No cardiomegaly. No clinically significant pericardial effusion. No coronary artery calcification.

Great vessels: The thoracic aorta is not dilated, with wall calcifications. The main pulmonary artery is not dilated.

Osseous structures and chest wall: Moderate degenerative changes of the thoracic spine with formation of small osteophytes. Decreased density of bone tissue in the vertebrae T11-L2 (not below 100 HU).

Included portions of the abdomen: Calcification in the right hepatic lobe up to 5 mm (no interval change).

The thyroid gland is within the scan range and is asymmetric (left greater than right), with heterogeneous structure due to heterogeneous nodules; the largest on the left is up to 2.9 × 2.7 cm (no interval change), with mild extension into the superior mediastinum.
rad_impression_en:
1.

Nodules in the left lower lobe forming a “tree-in-bud” pattern (Fleischner Society recommendations may not be applicable considering the clinical history), compared with the prior study show decreased nodule size (may correspond to sequelae of previously diagnosed coccidioidomycosis).

2.

Single stable solid and partially calcified pulmonary nodules. Per Fleischner Society recommendations 2017, no further follow-up is required.

3.

Single calcifications in the left lung.

4.

Atherosclerosis of the thoracic aorta.

5.

Stable partially calcified mediastinal lymph nodes, likely postinflammatory, including of specific etiology.

6.

Stable nodular thyroid changes within the scan range. Per Managing Incidental Thyroid Nodules Detected on Imaging: White Paper of the ACR Incidental Thyroid Findings Committee 2014, thyroid ultrasound is recommended.

7.

Moderate degenerative changes of the thoracic spine with formation of small osteophytes. CT findings of osteopenia of the T11-L2 vertebrae.
val_findings_en:
Lungs and pleura: Clustered centrilobular nodules persist in the left lower lobe, forming a “tree-in-bud” pattern, compared with the prior study with decreased size:
- The largest nodule previously up to 1.3 × 0.8 cm (series 5, image 112) has decreased to 6 mm on the current study, is less rounded, and has more linear margins.
- The largest nodule on the current study is up to 8 mm (series 5, image 104), previously up to 9 mm.
The remaining previously noted solid and calcified nodules (single) show no interval change, shown on series 5:
In the right lung:
- In the upper lobe, a subpleural nodule up to 3 mm, partially calcified (image 68).
- In the middle lobe, a subpleural nodule up to 3 mm (image 103).
Single calcifications in the left upper lobe up to 5 mm (no interval change).
No pleural effusion.

Mediastinum and lung hila: Partly calcified aortopulmonary window lymph node measures 1.5 cm in short axis,  no interval change. nullnlarged lower paratracheal lymph node with single calcifications, no interval change.

Heart: No cardiomegaly. No clinically significant pericardial effusion. No coronary artery calcification.

Great vessels: The thoracic aorta is not dilated, with mild wall calcifications. The main pulmonary artery is not dilated.

Osseous structures and chest wall: Moderate degenerative changes of the thoracic spine with formation of small osteophytes. Decrease in vertebral body density: T11 - 148 HU, T12 - 129 HU, L1 - 101 HU, L2 - 100 HU.

Included portions of the abdomen: Calcification in the right hepatic lobe up to 5 mm (no interval change). No need for further follow-up.

Thyroid: heterogeneous structure due to nodules; the largest on the left is up to 2.9 × 2.7 cm (no interval change), with mild extension into the superior mediastinum. Further evaluation with thyroid ultrasound recommended if not already obtained elsewhere.
val_impression_en:
1. Decreased in size, clustered centrilobular nodules in the left lower lobe, consistent with sequelae of known coccidiomycosis. No new pulmonary nodules. Stable solid and partially calcified pulmonary nodules. Per Fleischner Society recommendations 2017, no further follow-up is required.

2. Mild atherosclerosis of the thoracic aorta. No coronary artery calcification.

3. Stable partly calcified mediastinal lymph nodes, sequelae of granulomatous disease.

4. Stable thyroid nodules. Further evaluation with thyroid ultrasound recommended if not already obtained elsewhere.

5. Moderate degenerative changes of the thoracic spine. Decreased vertebral body density consistent with osteopenia.

rad_findings_en:
Lungs and pleura:  
The right hemidiaphragm is elevated, the basilar segments of the right lung are compressed, and aeration of the right lower lobe is decreased (visually without significant change). Less pronounced linear atelectasis is present in the right middle lobe and in the basal segments on the left (visually unchanged).  
In the right lower lobe (series 5, image 127), a subpleural focal area of consolidation persists, measuring about 2.5 × 1.3 cm (previously about 2.4 × 1.3 cm), with band-like margins, with small air lucencies up to 4 mm (previously up to 3 mm).  

In the aerated portions of the left lung, solitary solid nodules persist in the same number (series 5):  
In the upper lobe:  
* Subpleural nodule measuring up to 6 mm (image 59), with calcification, unchanged.  
In the lower lobe:  
* Subpleural nodule measuring up to 5 mm (image 161), with calcification, unchanged.  
* Subpleural nodule measuring up to 4 mm (image 187), with calcification, previously up to 3 mm.  

Solitary calcifications are present in the lungs, the largest in the right upper lobe measuring up to 5 mm.  
The right costal pleura at the level of the posterior segment of the 3rd rib/intercostal space shows focal thickening up to 9 mm (unchanged).  
No pleural effusion.  

Mild atelectasis is noted in the right lower lobe and an infiltrate in a ground-glass pattern. Focal pleural-based parenchymal thickening in the mediobasal part of the right lower lobe measures 1.7 cm. Additionally, hypodense renal nodules are noted in the costophrenic angle. In the lateral part of the mid zone of the left lung, a pleural-based pleural nodule measuring 6 mm is also identified and is stable.  

Mediastinum and pulmonary hila: No mass or lymphadenopathy is identified.  

Heart: The cardiac chambers are mildly enlarged. No clinically significant pericardial effusion. Mild coronary calcification.  

Great vessels: The thoracic aorta is dilated in the ascending portion up to 4.6 cm (unchanged), the descending aorta is not dilated, and the walls are calcified. The pulmonary trunk is dilated up to 3.4 cm (unchanged by the author’s measurements).  

Osseous structures and chest wall: Decreased height of the T12 vertebral body up to 32%, decreased density of vertebral bodies T11 and L1 to +84 HU (CT signs of osteoporosis). Moderate degenerative changes of the thoracic spine with small osteophytes.  

Included portions of the abdomen are unchanged: Numerous hypodense lesions in the liver with fluid density, the largest at the border of segments 5 and 6 measuring up to 4.1 × 3.3 cm; closer to the paracaval portions of segment 5, a solitary lesion with a peripheral calcification up to 3 mm (series 4, image 211).  
In the lateral limb of the left adrenal gland, a low-density nodular structure (-5 HU) measures about 9 × 9 mm (series 4, image 235).
rad_impression_en:
1. Subpleural focal consolidation in the right lower lobe may correspond to rounded atelectasis; against this background, the presence or absence of malignant changes cannot be excluded. Continue CT follow-up in 6–12 months. No significant change compared with the prior study. Focal thickening of the right costal pleura at the level of the right upper lobe is unchanged (postinflammatory changes versus; correlate with clinical history).  

2. Solitary solid partially calcified nodules in the left lung are unchanged (likely postinflammatory). If risk factors are present, follow-up chest CT may be performed in 12 months; if no such risk factors are present, per 2017 Fleischner Society recommendations, no further follow-up is required.  

3. Dilatation of the pulmonary trunk up to 3.4 cm. This may be a sign of pulmonary hypertension.  

4. Cardiomegaly and mild coronary calcification. The patient is advised to consult with their primary care physician for consideration of preventive risk modification or quantitative ordinal assessment of coronary artery calcification for each of the four main coronary arteries.  

5. Aortic valve calcification.  

6. Aneurysmal dilatation of the ascending thoracic aorta (unchanged) and atherosclerosis of the thoracic aorta.  

7. CT signs of osteoporosis (T11–L1). Moderate degenerative changes of the thoracic spine.  

8. Multiple hypodense liver lesions with fluid structure (may correspond to cysts; in a noncontrast study, complete evaluation is not possible), unchanged. Per Management of Incidental Liver Lesions on CT: A White Paper of the ACR Incidental Findings Committee 2017, if no risk factors are present, follow-up is not required.  

9. Small left adrenal nodule with a benign phenotype. Per Management of Incidental Adrenal Masses: A White Paper of the ACR Incidental Findings Committee 2017, follow-up is not required.
val_findings_en:
Lungs and pleura: The right hemidiaphragm is elevated, the basilar segments of the right lung are compressed, several discoid atelectases in the lower lobe, unchanged ground glass area with reticulations supradiaphragmatically. Discoid atelectases are present in the middle lobe and in the left lung. Reticular (fibrous) changes with traction bronchioloectasis in the dorsal parts of both lungs, unchanged.
In the right lower lobe (series 5, image 127), a subpleural area of consolidation measures about 2.2 × 1.1 cm (previously about 1.6 × 1.0 cm), with irregular margins, with gas collections up to 4 mm (previously up to 3 mm).
Solid nodules are presented as follows(series 5):  
In the lower right lobe:  
* Dorsally, against the background of reticulation (fibrosis), multiple nodules (image 83, 97, 102, 122, 129), unchanged.  
* Cavitating nodules measuring up to 6 mm (image 131, 137, 143), unchanged,  may be pseudocavities. 
In the upper left lobe:  
* Subpleural nodule measuring up to 6 mm (image 59), with calcification, unchanged.  
In the lower left lobe:  
* Subpleural nodule measuring up to 5 mm (image 161), with calcification, unchanged.  
* Subpleural nodule measuring up to 4 mm (image 187), with calcification, unchanged.  
Calcified nodules are present in both lungs, the largest in the right upper lobe measuring up to 5 mm.  
The right costal pleura at the level of the posterior segment of the 3rd rib/intercostal space shows focal thickening up to 9 mm (unchanged).  
No pleural effusion.  

Mediastinum and pulmonary hila: No mass or lymphadenopathy is identified.  

Heart: The left cardiac chambers are mildly enlarged. No clinically significant pericardial effusion. Mild coronary calcification. Mild calcifications of the aortic and mitral valves.

Great vessels: The thoracic aorta is dilated in the ascending portion up to 4.6 cm (unchanged), the descending aorta is not dilated, and the walls are calcified. The pulmonary trunk is dilated up to 3.4 cm (unchanged).  

Osseous structures and chest wall: Decreased height of the T12 vertebral body up to 32%, decreased density of vertebral bodies T11 and L1 to +84 HU. Moderate degenerative changes of the thoracic spine with small osteophytes.  

Included portions of the abdomen: Numerous stable hypodense lesions in the liver with fluid density, the largest at the border of segments 5 and 6 measuring up to 4.1 × 3.3 cm; closer to the paracaval portions of segment 5, a solitary lesion with a peripheral calcification up to 3 mm (series 4, image 211).
val_impression_en:
1. In the right lower lobe, subpleural cavitating pulmonary mass with interval growth in size. Interval growth raises concern for a possible neoplastic etiology, inflammatory changes are less likely. Multiple solid, calcified, cavitating nodules, unchanged. Interstitial changes in the lower lobes, more pronounced on the right.

2. Aneurysmal dilatation of the ascending thoracic aorta (unchanged). Atherosclerosis of the thoracic aorta.  

3. Dilatation of the pulmonary trunk up to 3.4 cm. This may be a sign of pulmonary hypertension.  

4. Left heart enlargement. Mild coronary calcification. Mild calcifications of the aortic and mitral valves.

5. Wedge deformity of the T12 vertebral body and decreased vertebral body density consistent with osteoporosis. Moderate degenerative changes of the thoracic spine.  

6. Multiple hypodense liver lesions with fluid structure (may correspond to cysts), unchanged. Per Management of Incidental Liver Lesions on CT: A White Paper of the ACR Incidental Findings Committee 2017, if no risk factors are present, follow-up is not required.

rad_findings_en:
FINDINGS:
Lungs and pleura: Pulmonary nodules in series 5 are as follows:
Left upper lobe:
- A 6 mm nodule on image 99.
- A 4 mm nodule on image 94.
- A 2 mm nodule on image 89.
- A 2 mm nodule on image 145.
- A 2 mm nodule on image 143.
- A 2 mm nodule on image 88.
Right upper lobe:
- A 3 mm nodule on image 68.
- A 2 mm nodule on image 99.
Right middle lobe:
- A 2 mm nodule on image 142.
Air trapping in the posterior lungs. Calcified granulomas in the right lower lobe (numerous, clustered), and in the left upper and lower lobes (a few) measuring up to 4 mm. A single linear atelectasis in the right lower lobe. No pleural effusion on the right; small left pleural effusion.
Mediastinum and pulmonary hila: No mass and no lymphadenopathy. Calcifications within a single right station 11 lymph node.
Heart: Moderately enlarged. No clinically significant pericardial effusion. No coronary calcification.
Great vessels: The thoracic aorta is not dilated, with a single focus of wall calcification.
Skeletal structures and chest wall: Decreased density of vertebral bodies: T11 - 136 HU, T12 - 123 HU, L1 - 115 HU. Moderate degenerative changes of the thoracic spine. Healing fracture of the right 4th rib. Enostosis in the head of the right humerus measuring up to 5 mm in diameter.
Upper abdomen: A lesion of the medial crus of the left adrenal gland measuring 8 mm × 12.0 cm, likely an adenoma. Multiple splenic calcifications measuring up to 4 mm. In liver segment 6, a calcification measuring 4 × 6 mm.
rad_impression_en:
1. Bilateral pulmonary nodules. ACR Fleischner Society recommendations (MacMahon, et al. Radiology 2017; 284(1): 228-43) suggest the following: For patients with low risk of lung cancer, initial chest CT at 3-6 months. If stable, no further follow-up or an optional chest CT at 18-24 months if there is high index of suspicion. For patients with high risk of lung cancer, initial chest CT at 3-6 months. If stable, another chest CT at 18-24 months.

2. Calcified granulomas and a single calcified lymph node (possibly related to a prior granulomatous process).

3. Small left pleural effusion.

4. Left adrenal lesion. ACR White Paper recommendations (Mayo-Smith, et al. J Am Coll Radiol 2017;14:1038-1044) suggest the following: Benign adenoma. No need for further imaging follow-up. Consider biochemical evaluation to determine functional status.

5. Atherosclerosis of the thoracic aorta.

6. Moderate cardiomegaly.

7. Decreased vertebral body density (CT signs of demineralization).

8. Liver calcification.

9. Splenic calcifications.

10. Moderate degenerative changes of the thoracic spine.

DISCLAIMER: AI lung nodule detection was utilized on this study. This system is designed for high sensitivity, potentially identifying benign findings. Please refer to the radiologist's interpretation to assess the clinical significance of any detected findings.
val_findings_en:
Lungs and pleura: Pulmonary nodules in series 5 are as follows:
In the right upper lobe:
- A 3 mm solid nodule on image 68.
- A 2 mm solid nodule on image 99.
In the right middle lobe:
- A 2 mm solid nodule on image 142.
In the left upper lobe:
- A 6 mm solid nodule on image 99.
- A 4 mm solid nodule on image 94.
- A 2 mm solid nodule on image 89.
- A 2 mm solid nodule on image 145.
- A 2 mm solid nodule on image 143.
- A 2 mm solid nodule on image 88.
Numerous, clustered calcified nodules in the right lower lobe, and a few in the left upper and lower lobes measure up to 4 mm. Air trapping in the lower lobes. Gravitational changes in the dorsal lungs. No pleural effusion.

Mediastinum and pulmonary hila: No mass and no lymphadenopathy. Calcifications within hilar and interlobar lymph nodes.

Heart: No cardiomegaly. Trace pericardial effusion with an effusion thickness of up to 8 mm. No coronary calcification.

Great vessels: The pulmonary arteries and thoracic aorta are of normal caliber. 

Skeletal structures and chest wall: Decreased density of vertebral bodies: T11 - 136 HU, T12 - 123 HU, L1 - 115 HU (CT signs of demineralization). Moderate degenerative changes of the thoracic spine. Healing fracture of the right 4th rib. Enostosis in the head of the right humerus measuring up to 5 mm in diameter.

Upper abdomen: A lesion of the medial crus of the left adrenal gland measuring 8 mm (adrenal lesion <1 cm in size in the short axis does not require follow-up according to Management of Incidental Adrenal Masses: A White Paper of the ACR Incidental Findings Committee, 2017). Multiple splenic calcifications measuring up to 4 mm, benign. In liver segment 6, a calcification measuring 4 × 6 mm was reported on ultrasound in 2024, probably benign.
val_impression_en:
1. Multiple bilateral solid nodules up to 6 mm. ACR Fleischner Society recommendations (MacMahon, et al. Radiology 2017; 284(1): 228-43) suggest the following: 
- For patients with low risk of lung cancer, initial chest CT at 3-6 months. If stable, no further follow-up or an optional chest CT at 18-24 months if there is high index of suspicion. 
- For patients with high risk of lung cancer, initial chest CT at 3-6 months. If stable, another chest CT at 18-24 months.

2. Multiple calcified nodules and calcified mediastinal lymph nodes correspond to a prior granulomatous process.

3. Trace pericardial effusion.

DISCLAIMER: AI lung nodule detection was utilized on this study. This system is designed for high sensitivity, potentially identifying benign findings. Please refer to the radiologist's interpretation to assess the clinical significance of any detected findings.

rad_findings_en:
Lungs and pleura: Pulmonary nodules in series 5 are as follows:
Right upper lobe:
- Solid subpleural nodule measuring 5 mm on image 53 (unchanged).
In both lungs, micronodules measuring up to 3 mm. In both lungs, discoid atelectasis. In the left upper lobe, a calcified granuloma measuring up to 2 mm. No pleural effusion.

Mediastinum and pulmonary hila: No mass or lymphadenopathy.

Heart: No cardiomegaly. Mild pericardial effusion measuring up to 6 mm in thickness. Coronary calcification is present in the left main, left anterior descending, left circumflex, and right coronary arteries, qualitatively assessed as severe. Aortic valve calcification.

Great vessels: Dilatation of the ascending thoracic aorta measuring up to 4.3 cm, with moderate wall calcification. The main pulmonary artery is dilated measuring up to 3.2 cm.

Skeletal structures and chest wall: Severe degenerative changes of the thoracic spine. Demineralization of bone. Healing fractures of the left ribs. Spinal instrumentation at T1-T2.

Upper abdomen: In the left kidney, a mass with density of 8 HU, measuring 6.6 × 7.8 cm (unchanged).
rad_impression_en:
1. Solid nodule in the right upper lobe and micronodules in both lungs are stable compared with March 21, 2025 and September 05, 2025. In accordance with 2017 Fleischner Society recommendations, additional CT follow-up is not recommended. Calcified granuloma in the left upper lobe is consistent with prior infection.

2. Mild pericardial effusion. Severe coronary calcification. The patient is advised to consult with their primary care physician for consideration of preventive risk modification or quantitative ordinal assessment of coronary artery calcification for each of the four main coronary arteries. Aortic valve calcification.

3. Dilated main pulmonary artery (3.2 cm), which may suggest pulmonary hypertension.

4. Dilatation of the ascending thoracic aorta. Moderate atherosclerosis of the thoracic aorta.

5. Demineralization of bone. Healing fractures of the left ribs. Spinal instrumentation at T1-T2.

6. Left renal mass. Per Management of the Incidental Renal Mass on CT: A White Paper of the ACR Incidental Findings Committee, 2018, no further follow-up is required.

DISCLAIMER: AI lung nodule detection was utilized on this study. This system is designed for high sensitivity, potentially identifying benign findings. Please refer to the radiologist's interpretation to assess the clinical significance of any detected findings.
val_findings_en:
Lungs and pleura: Pulmonary nodules in series 5 are as follows:
Right lower lobe:
- Solid nodule measures 2 mm on image 135 (unchanged).
- Previously identified on image 162 on Chest CT on March 21, 2025, solid nodule is resolved.
Left upper lobe:
- Calcified nodule measures 3 mm on image 67 (unchanged), calcified granuloma.
- Ground-glass nodule measures 3 mm on image 61 (unchanged).
Left lower lobe:
- Solid nodule measures 2 mm on image 69 (unchanged).
- Solid nodule measures 2 mm on image 90 (unchanged).
- Ground-glass nodule measures 3 mm on image 77 (unchanged).
In the right lower lobe, a group of micronodules (tree-in-bud) on images 157-169. Compared to Chest CT on March 21, 2025, resolution of peripheral ground-glass opacity is noted. Linear atelectasis in the right middle lobe, left lingula, and bilateral basal segments, gravity-dependent atelectasis. No pleural effusion.

Mediastinum and pulmonary hila: No mass or lymphadenopathy.

Heart: No cardiomegaly. Trace pericardial effusion measuring up to 6 mm in thickness. Coronary calcification is present in the left main, left anterior descending, left circumflex, and right coronary arteries, qualitatively assessed as severe. Aortic and mitral valve calcification.

Great vessels: Dilatation of the ascending thoracic aorta measuring up to 4.3 cm, with moderate wall calcification. The main pulmonary artery is dilated measuring up to 3.2 cm.

Skeletal structures and chest wall: Moderate degenerative changes of the thoracic spine. Multiple healed rib fractures.

Upper abdomen: In the left kidney, a mass with density of 8 HU, measuring 6.6 × 7.8 cm (unchanged).
val_impression_en:
1. Solid and ground-glass nodules in both lungs are stable compared with March 21, 2025. For ground-glass nodules, ACR Fleischner Society recommendations (MacMahon, et al. Radiology 2017; 284(1): 228-43) suggest the following: Follow-up chest CT at 2 and 4 years after the initial discovery of the nodules. For stable solid nodules, no further follow-up is recommended. Calcified granuloma in the left upper lobe is consistent with prior infection. Partially resolved bronchiolitis in the right lower lobe.

2. Severe coronary calcification. The patient is advised to consult with their primary care physician for consideration of preventive risk modification or quantitative ordinal assessment of coronary artery calcification for each of the four main coronary arteries.

3. Dilated main pulmonary artery (3.2 cm), which may suggest pulmonary hypertension.

4. Trace pericardial effusion.

5. Dilatation of the ascending thoracic aorta. Moderate atherosclerosis of the thoracic aorta. Aortic and mitral valve calcification.

6. Left renal cyst. Per Management of the Incidental Renal Mass on CT: A White Paper of the ACR Incidental Findings Committee, 2018, no further follow-up is required.

DISCLAIMER: AI lung nodule detection was utilized on this study. This system is designed for high sensitivity, potentially identifying benign findings. Please refer to the radiologist's interpretation to assess the clinical significance of any detected findings.
